revision.text:
[[File:Takase Shrine torii.jpeg|thumb|right|Torii at Takase-jinja]] {{nihongo|'''Takase-jinja'''|高瀬神社||}} is a [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] at [[Nanto, Toyama|Nanto]] in [[Toyama Prefecture]].<ref name="kotodamaya">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/takase-jinja/ "Takase Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref>
==History== The shrine was established before the 10th century.
This place is special to the ''[[kami]]'' named Takase-no-kami<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [http://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA371&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 371].</ref> or {{nihongo|Ookuninushi no ookami|大国主神||}}.<ref name="kotodamaya"/>
Takase was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of the old [[Etchū Province]]. It serves today as one of the ''ichinomiya'' of [[Toyama Prefecture]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref>
In the modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines, Watatsu was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}}.
==References== {{reflist}}
==Other websites == {{Commons category-inline|Taksase-jinja}} * [http://www.takase.or.jp/ Takase jinja website {{ja icon}}]
<!-- {{coord|34|18|70|N|136|56|53.70|E|source:___|display=title}} -->
[[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Japan]]
[[ja:高瀬神社]]

[[File:Takase Shrine torii.jpeg|thumb|right|Torii at Takase-jinja]] {{nihongo|'''Takase-jinja'''|高瀬神社||}} is a [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] at [[Nanto, Toyama|Nanto]] in [[Toyama Prefecture]].<ref name="kotodamaya">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/takase-jinja/ "Takase Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref>
==History== This place is special to the ''[[kami]]'' named Takase-no-kami<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [http://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA371&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 371].</ref> or {{nihongo|Ookuninushi no ookami|大国主神||}}.<ref name="kotodamaya"/>
Takase was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of the old [[Etchū Province]]. It serves today as one of the ''ichinomiya'' of [[Toyama Prefecture]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref>
In the modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines, Watatsu was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}}.
==References== {{reflist}}
==Other websites == {{Commons category-inline|Taksase-jinja}} * [http://www.takase.or.jp/ Takase jinja website {{ja icon}}]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Takase Shrine
高瀬神社 | image = Takase Shrine torii.jpeg | width = 250 | caption = Torii at Takase Shrine | type = | dedication = [[Ōkuninushi]] | founded = pre-[[Nara period]] | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = Nanto-shi, Toyama-ken | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Takase Shrine'''|高瀬神社|Takase-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in the city of [[Nanto, Toyama| Nanto]], [[Toyama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It one of several shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Etchū Province]][http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2013-1-25.. The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined is [[Ōkuninushi]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on September 13. ==History== The original construction of this shrine is unknown, but it pre-dates the [[Nara period]]. The shrine claims to have been founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Keikō]][http://www.takase.or.jp/guide.html#3] official home page The first mention of the shrine in historical records is in an entry in the 780 AD ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', and in the 795 AD ''[[Nihon Kōki]]''. By the 859 AD ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it had been accorded 3rd Court Rank. This place is special to the ''[[kami]]'' named Takase-no-kamiPickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA371&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 371]. or {{nihongo|Ōkuninushi no ōkami|大国主神||}}.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/takase-jinja/ "Takase Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-25. It was patronised by [[Maeda clan]] during the [[Edo period]]. Under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], the shrine was classified as a National Shrine of the Third Rank (''Kokuhei Shōsha'' (国幣小社)). ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Takase-jinja}} * {{Official website|http://www.takase.or.jp}} {{coord|36|34|18.7|N|136|56|53.7|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Toyama Prefecture]] [[Category:Etchū Province]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Takase Shrine
高瀬神社 | image = Takase Shrine torii.jpeg | width = 250 | caption = Torii at Takase Shrine | type = | dedication = [[Ōkuninushi]] | founded = pre-[[Nara period]] | closed = | founder = | priest = | location_map = Japan | coordinates ={{coord|36|34|18.7|N|136|56|53.7|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | address = Nanto-shi, Toyama-ken | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Takase Shrine'''|高瀬神社|Takase-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in the city of [[Nanto, Toyama| Nanto]], [[Toyama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined is [[Ōkuninushi]]. By the 859 AD ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it had been accorded 3rd Court Rank. This place is special to the ''[[kami]]'' named Takase-no-kamiPickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA371&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 371]. or {{nihongo|Ōkuninushi no ōkami|大国主神||}}.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/takase-jinja/ "Takase Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-25. It was patronised by [[Maeda clan]] during the [[Edo period]]. Under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], the shrine was classified as a National Shrine of the Third Rank (''Kokuhei Shōsha'' (国幣小社)). ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Takase-jinja}} * {{Official website|http://www.takase.or.jp}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Toyama Prefecture]] [[Category:Etchū Province]]

Under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], the shrine was classified as a National Shrine of the Third Rank (''Kokuhei Shōsha'' (国幣小社)). ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Takase-jinja}} * {{Official website|http://www.takase.or.jp}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Toyama Prefecture]] [[Category:Etchū Province]] [[Category:Nanto, Toyama]]

Under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], the shrine was classified as a National Shrine of the Third Rank (''Kokuhei Shōsha'' (国幣小社)). ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Takase-jinja}} * {{Official website|http://www.takase.or.jp}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Toyama Prefecture]] [[Category:Etchū Province]] [[Category:Nanto, Toyama]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Takase Shrine
高瀬神社 | image = Takase Shrine torii.jpeg | caption = Torii at Takase Shrine | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|36|34|18.7|N|136|56|53.7|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]] | founded_by = | established = pre-[[Nara period]] | date_destroyed = | location = Nanto-shi, Toyama-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Takase Shrine'''|高瀬神社|Takase-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in the city of [[Nanto, Toyama|Nanto]], [[Toyama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It one of several shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Etchū Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2013-1-25. The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined is [[Ōkuninushi]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on September 13. ==History== The original construction of this shrine is unknown, but it pre-dates the [[Nara period]]. The shrine claims to have been founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Keikō]][http://www.takase.or.jp/guide.html#3] official home page The first mention of the shrine in historical records is in an entry in the 780 AD ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', and in the 795 AD ''[[Nihon Kōki]]''. By the 859 AD ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it had been accorded 3rd Court Rank. This place is special to the ''[[kami]]'' named Takase-no-kamiPickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA371&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 371]. or {{nihongo|Ōkuninushi no ōkami|大国主神||}}.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/takase-jinja/ "Takase Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-25. It was patronised by [[Maeda clan]] during the [[Edo period]]. Under the pre-World War II [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], the shrine was classified as a National Shrine of the Third Rank (''Kokuhei Shōsha'' (国幣小社)). ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Takase-jinja}} * {{Official website|http://www.takase.or.jp}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Toyama Prefecture]] [[Category:Etchū Province]] [[Category:Nanto, Toyama]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Takase Shrine |native_name = 高瀬神社 | image = Takase Shrine torii.jpeg | caption = Torii at Takase Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|36|34|18.7|N|136|56|53.7|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Takase Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]] | founded_by = | established = pre-[[Nara period]] | location = 291 Takase, Nanto-shi, Toyama-ken 932-0252 | website = {{Official website|https://www.takase.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 13 }} {{nihongo|'''Takase Shrine'''|高瀬神社|Takase-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Takase neighborhood of the city of [[Nanto, Toyama|Nanto]], [[Toyama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It one of four shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Etchū Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref> The shrine's main festival is held annually on September 13.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takase Jinja are: *{{nihongo|'''Ōkuninushi'''|大己貴命|}}<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA371&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 371].</ref>.<ref name="kotodamaya">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/takase-jinja/ "Takase Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref> *{{nihongo|'''Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto'''|天活玉命|}} *{{nihongo|'''Isotakeru-no-kami'''|五十猛神|}} [[Category:Beppo Shrines]]
==History== The original construction of Takase Jinja is uncertain. Worship of [[Ōkuninushi]] was widespread in the [[Hokuriku region]] from before the [[Nara period]] and is believed to have been brought to this area by migrants from [[Izumo Province|Izumo]]. The shrine claims to have been founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Keikō]] (reigned 71–130 AD) <ref>[http://www.takase.or.jp/guide.html#3] official home page</ref>
The first mention of the shrine in historical records is in an entry in the 780 AD ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the 795 AD ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' it was promoted to Junior 5th Rank, Upper Grade, and the 840 AD ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' states that it was promoted from Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade to Junior 4th Rank, Upper Grade, and per the 854 AD ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', it had been accorded Junior 3rd Rank. Per the 859 AD ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it had advanced to Senior 3rd Rank. While these are indications of its high status, in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD it is listed as only one of seven minor shrines in Tonami County of Etchū Province. However, late in the [[Heian period]], the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Etchū ws relocated to Tonami, and the Takase Jinja was elevated to an ''ichinomiya'' and was greatly enlarged. Under the ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' movement joining Shintoism and Buddhism, it once had 300 Buddhist chapels. This period of prosperity was short-lived. With the growth of the ''[[Ikkō-shū]]'' movement in Etchū and the various battles of the [[Sengoku period]], the area around the Takase Jinja was devastated, and it fell largely into ruin. After the establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the creation of [[Toyama Domain]], the shrine was revived with the patronage of the [[Maeda clan]]. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designed as a "prefectural shrine" under the a [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], but was later raised in status to a {{nihongo|National Shrine,3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shōsha}} in 1923. <ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is located a 40 minute walk from [[Fukuno Station (Toyama)|Fukuno Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Jōhana Line]].<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=4901398067}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Takase Shrine haiden.jpeg |''Haiden'' File:国幣小社高瀬神社.jpg|Takase Jinja in 1938 File:県社高瀬神社.jpg|Takase Jinja in 1909 </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Ichinomiya]]

After the establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the creation of [[Toyama Domain]], the shrine was revived with the patronage of the [[Maeda clan]]. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designed as a "prefectural shrine" under the a [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]], but was later raised in status to a {{nihongo|National Shrine,3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shōsha}} in 1923. <ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is located a 40 minute walk from [[Fukuno Station (Toyama)|Fukuno Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Jōhana Line]].<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=4901398067}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Takase Shrine haiden.jpeg |''Haiden'' File:国幣小社高瀬神社.jpg|Takase Jinja in 1938 File:県社高瀬神社.jpg|Takase Jinja in 1909 </gallery>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高瀬神社 (守口市)]] {{distinguish|Takase Shrine (Q3791906)}}
'''Takase Shrine (Q11671973)''' is a shrine in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Kawachi Province]].
== Shikinaisha (式内社) == {| class="wikitable sortable" ! District !! Name !! Funding category !! Rank !! Notes !! Same as !! Co-ords !! Shrine DB |- | {{ill|Matta district|ja|茨田郡|zh|茨田郡|WD=Q11617933}} || {{ill|Takase Shrine|lt=Takase Shrine|ja|高瀬神社 (守口市)|WD=Q11671973}} || {{ill|Kanpei-sha (Engishiki Jinmyocho)|WD=Q135160338|lt=Kanpei|comment=categorized}}[[Category:Kanpei-sha (Engishiki Jinmyocho)]] || {{ill|Shikinai Shosha|WD=Q134917287}} || — || — || {{coord|34.729323|135.562611|display=inline}} || [https://jmapps.ne.jp/kokugakuin/det.html?data_id=180925 DB] |} ==Overview==
{{Short description|Shinto shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takase Shrine |native_name = 高瀬神社 | image = Takase Shrine torii.jpeg | caption = Torii at Takase Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|36|34|18.7|N|136|56|53.7|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Takase Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]] | founded_by = | established = pre-[[Nara period]] | location = 291 Takase, Nanto-shi, Toyama-ken 932-0252 | website = {{Official website|https://www.takase.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 13 }} {{nihongo|'''Takase Shrine'''|高瀬神社|Takase-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Takase neighborhood of the city of [[Nanto, Toyama|Nanto]], [[Toyama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It one of four shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Etchū Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref> The shrine's main festival is held annually on September 13.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takase Jinja are: *{{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''|大己貴命|}}.<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA371&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 371].</ref><ref name="kotodamaya">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/takase-jinja/ "Takase Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref> *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=}}'''|天活玉命|}} *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-kami|ja|五十猛神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isotakeru-no-kami|qq=}}'''|五十猛神|}}
==History== The original construction of Takase Jinja is uncertain. Worship of [[Ōkuninushi]] was widespread in the [[Hokuriku region]] from before the [[Nara period]] and is believed to have been brought to this area by migrants from [[Izumo Province|Izumo]]. The shrine claims to have been founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Keikō]] (reigned 71–130 AD) <ref>[http://www.takase.or.jp/guide.html#3] official home page</ref>
The first mention of the shrine in historical records is in an entry in the 780 AD ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the 795 AD ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' it was promoted to Junior 5th Rank, Upper Grade, and the 840 AD ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' states that it was promoted from Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade to Junior 4th Rank, Upper Grade, and per the 854 AD ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', it had been accorded Junior 3rd Rank. Per the 859 AD ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it had advanced to Senior 3rd Rank. While these are indications of its high status, in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD it is listed as only one of seven minor shrines in Tonami County of Etchū Province. However, late in the [[Heian period]], the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Etchū was relocated to Tonami, and the Takase Jinja was elevated to an ''ichinomiya'' and was greatly enlarged. Under the ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' movement joining Shintoism and Buddhism, it once had 300 Buddhist chapels. This period of prosperity was short-lived. With the growth of the ''[[Ikkō-shū]]'' movement in Etchū and the various battles of the [[Sengoku period]], the area around the Takase Jinja was devastated, and it fell largely into ruin. After the establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the creation of [[Toyama Domain]], the shrine was revived with the patronage of the [[Maeda clan]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designed as a "prefectural shrine" under the a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], but was later raised in status to a {{nihongo|National Shrine,3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1923.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is located a 40 minute walk from [[Fukuno Station (Toyama)|Fukuno Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Jōhana Line]].<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Takase Shrine haiden.jpeg |''Haiden'' File:国幣小社高瀬神社.jpg|Takase Jinja in 1938 File:県社高瀬神社.jpg|Takase Jinja in 1909 </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Ichinomiya]] * Other shrines claiming to be [[Etchū Province|Etchū]] Ichinomiya ** {{ill|Imizu Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imizu Shrine|qq=}} ** {{ill|Keta Jinja|lt=Keta Jinja|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keta Jinja|qq=}} ** {{ill|Oyama Shrine (Tateyama)|lt=Oyama Shrine|simple|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyama Shrine (Tateyama)|qq=}}
==References== {{reflist}}

== Accidentally Overwritten Content == [[Category:Pages with merged translations]][[Category:Pages with recovered content]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takase Shrine |native_name = 高瀬神社 | image = Takase Shrine torii.jpeg | caption = Torii at Takase Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|36|34|18.7|N|136|56|53.7|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Takase Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]] | founded_by = | established = pre-[[Nara period]] | location = 291 Takase, Nanto-shi, Toyama-ken 932-0252 | website = {{Official website|https://www.takase.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 13 }} {{nihongo|'''Takase Shrine'''|高瀬神社|Takase-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Takase neighborhood of the city of [[Nanto, Toyama|Nanto]], [[Toyama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It one of four shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Etchū Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref> The shrine's main festival is held annually on September 13.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takase Jinja are: *{{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''|大己貴命|}}.<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA371&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 371].</ref><ref name="kotodamaya">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/takase-jinja/ "Takase Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref> *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=}}'''|天活玉命|}} *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-kami|ja|五十猛神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isotakeru-no-kami|qq=}}'''|五十猛神|}}
==History== The original construction of Takase Jinja is uncertain. Worship of [[Ōkuninushi]] was widespread in the [[Hokuriku region]] from before the [[Nara period]] and is believed to have been brought to this area by migrants from [[Izumo Province|Izumo]]. The shrine claims to have been founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Keikō]] (reigned 71–130 AD) <ref>[http://www.takase.or.jp/guide.html#3] official home page</ref>
The first mention of the shrine in historical records is in an entry in the 780 AD ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the 795 AD ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' it was promoted to Junior 5th Rank, Upper Grade, and the 840 AD ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' states that it was promoted from Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade to Junior 4th Rank, Upper Grade, and per the 854 AD ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', it had been accorded Junior 3rd Rank. Per the 859 AD ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it had advanced to Senior 3rd Rank. While these are indications of its high status, in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD it is listed as only one of seven minor shrines in Tonami County of Etchū Province. However, late in the [[Heian period]], the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Etchū was relocated to Tonami, and the Takase Jinja was elevated to an ''ichinomiya'' and was greatly enlarged. Under the ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' movement joining Shintoism and Buddhism, it once had 300 Buddhist chapels. This period of prosperity was short-lived. With the growth of the ''[[Ikkō-shū]]'' movement in Etchū and the various battles of the [[Sengoku period]], the area around the Takase Jinja was devastated, and it fell largely into ruin. After the establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the creation of [[Toyama Domain]], the shrine was revived with the patronage of the [[Maeda clan]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designed as a "prefectural shrine" under the a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], but was later raised in status to a {{nihongo|National Shrine,3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1923.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is located a 40 minute walk from [[Fukuno Station (Toyama)|Fukuno Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Jōhana Line]].<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Takase Shrine haiden.jpeg |''Haiden'' File:国幣小社高瀬神社.jpg|Takase Jinja in 1938 File:県社高瀬神社.jpg|Takase Jinja in 1909 </gallery>

== Accidentally Overwritten Content == [[Category:Pages with merged translations]][[Category:Pages with recovered content]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takase Shrine |native_name = 高瀬神社 | image = Takase Shrine torii.jpeg | caption = Torii at Takase Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|36|34|18.7|N|136|56|53.7|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Takase Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]] | founded_by = | established = pre-[[Nara period]] | location = 291 Takase, Nanto-shi, Toyama-ken 932-0252 | website = {{Official website|https://www.takase.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 13 }} {{nihongo|'''Takase Shrine'''|高瀬神社|Takase-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Takase neighborhood of the city of [[Nanto, Toyama|Nanto]], [[Toyama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It one of four shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Etchū Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref> The shrine's main festival is held annually on September 13.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takase Jinja are: *{{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''|大己貴命|}}.<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA371&dq= ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 371].</ref><ref name="kotodamaya">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/takase-jinja/ "Takase Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref> *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=}}'''|天活玉命|}} *{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-kami|ja|五十猛神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isotakeru-no-kami|qq=}}'''|五十猛神|}}
==History== The original construction of Takase Jinja is uncertain. Worship of [[Ōkuninushi]] was widespread in the [[Hokuriku region]] from before the [[Nara period]] and is believed to have been brought to this area by migrants from [[Izumo Province|Izumo]]. The shrine claims to have been founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Keikō]] (reigned 71–130 AD) <ref>[http://www.takase.or.jp/guide.html#3] official home page</ref>
The first mention of the shrine in historical records is in an entry in the 780 AD ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the 795 AD ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' it was promoted to Junior 5th Rank, Upper Grade, and the 840 AD ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' states that it was promoted from Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade to Junior 4th Rank, Upper Grade, and per the 854 AD ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', it had been accorded Junior 3rd Rank. Per the 859 AD ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it had advanced to Senior 3rd Rank. While these are indications of its high status, in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD it is listed as only one of seven minor shrines in Tonami County of Etchū Province. However, late in the [[Heian period]], the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Etchū was relocated to Tonami, and the Takase Jinja was elevated to an ''ichinomiya'' and was greatly enlarged. Under the ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' movement joining Shintoism and Buddhism, it once had 300 Buddhist chapels. This period of prosperity was short-lived. With the growth of the ''[[Ikkō-shū]]'' movement in Etchū and the various battles of the [[Sengoku period]], the area around the Takase Jinja was devastated, and it fell largely into ruin. After the establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the creation of [[Toyama Domain]], the shrine was revived with the patronage of the [[Maeda clan]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designed as a "prefectural shrine" under the a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], but was later raised in status to a {{nihongo|National Shrine,3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1923.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The shrine is located a 40 minute walk from [[Fukuno Station (Toyama)|Fukuno Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Jōhana Line]].<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Takase Shrine haiden.jpeg |''Haiden'' File:国幣小社高瀬神社.jpg|Takase Jinja in 1938 File:県社高瀬神社.jpg|Takase Jinja in 1909 </gallery>

{{distinguish|Takase Shrine (Q3791906)}}

'''Takase Shrine (Q11671973)''' is a shrine in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Kawachi Province]].

== Shikinaisha (式内社) ==

|}

{{nihongo|'''Takase Shrine'''|高瀬神社|Takase-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Takase neighborhood of the city of [[Nanto, Toyama|Nanto]], [[Toyama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It one of four shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Etchū Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref> The shrine's main festival is held annually on September 13.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

==Enshrined ''kami''==
The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takase Jinja are:
*{{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''|大己貴命|}}.<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA371&dq=  ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 371].</ref><ref name="kotodamaya">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/takase-jinja/ "Takase Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=}}'''|天活玉命|}}
*{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-kami|ja|五十猛神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isotakeru-no-kami|qq=}}'''|五十猛神|}}

The original construction of Takase Jinja is uncertain. Worship of [[Ōkuninushi]] was widespread in the [[Hokuriku region]] from before the [[Nara period]] and is believed to have been brought to this area by migrants from [[Izumo Province|Izumo]]. The shrine claims to have been founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Keikō]] (reigned 71–130 AD) <ref>[http://www.takase.or.jp/guide.html#3] official home page</ref>

The first mention of the shrine in historical records is in an entry in the 780 AD ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the 795 AD ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' it was promoted to Junior 5th Rank, Upper Grade, and the 840 AD ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' states that it was promoted from Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade to Junior 4th Rank, Upper Grade, and per the 854 AD ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', it had been accorded Junior 3rd Rank. Per the 859 AD ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it had advanced to Senior 3rd Rank. While these are indications of its high status, in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD it is listed as only one of seven minor shrines in Tonami County of Etchū Province. However, late in the [[Heian period]], the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Etchū was relocated to Tonami, and the Takase Jinja was elevated to an ''ichinomiya'' and was greatly enlarged. Under the ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' movement joining Shintoism and Buddhism, it once had 300 Buddhist chapels. This period of prosperity was short-lived. With the growth of the ''[[Ikkō-shū]]'' movement in Etchū and the various battles of the [[Sengoku period]], the area around the Takase Jinja was devastated, and it fell largely into ruin. After the establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the creation of [[Toyama Domain]], the shrine was revived with the patronage of the [[Maeda clan]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designed as a "prefectural shrine" under the a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], but was later raised in status to a {{nihongo|National Shrine,3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}  in 1923.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

The shrine is located a 40 minute walk from [[Fukuno Station (Toyama)|Fukuno Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Jōhana Line]].<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''Takase Shrine'''|高瀬神社|Takase-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Takase neighborhood of the city of [[Nanto, Toyama|Nanto]], [[Toyama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It one of four shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Etchū Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref> The shrine's main festival is held annually on September 13.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

==Enshrined ''kami''==
The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takase Jinja are:
*{{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''|大己貴命|}}.<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA371&dq=  ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 371].</ref><ref name="kotodamaya">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/takase-jinja/ "Takase Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=}}'''|天活玉命|}}
*{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-kami|ja|五十猛神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isotakeru-no-kami|qq=}}'''|五十猛神|}}

==History==
The original construction of Takase Jinja is uncertain. Worship of [[Ōkuninushi]] was widespread in the [[Hokuriku region]] from before the [[Nara period]] and is believed to have been brought to this area by migrants from [[Izumo Province|Izumo]]. The shrine claims to have been founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Keikō]] (reigned 71–130 AD) <ref>[http://www.takase.or.jp/guide.html#3] official home page</ref>

The first mention of the shrine in historical records is in an entry in the 780 AD ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the 795 AD ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' it was promoted to Junior 5th Rank, Upper Grade, and the 840 AD ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' states that it was promoted from Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade to Junior 4th Rank, Upper Grade, and per the 854 AD ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', it had been accorded Junior 3rd Rank. Per the 859 AD ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it had advanced to Senior 3rd Rank. While these are indications of its high status, in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD it is listed as only one of seven minor shrines in Tonami County of Etchū Province. However, late in the [[Heian period]], the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Etchū was relocated to Tonami, and the Takase Jinja was elevated to an ''ichinomiya'' and was greatly enlarged. Under the ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' movement joining Shintoism and Buddhism, it once had 300 Buddhist chapels. This period of prosperity was short-lived. With the growth of the ''[[Ikkō-shū]]'' movement in Etchū and the various battles of the [[Sengoku period]], the area around the Takase Jinja was devastated, and it fell largely into ruin. After the establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the creation of [[Toyama Domain]], the shrine was revived with the patronage of the [[Maeda clan]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designed as a "prefectural shrine" under the a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], but was later raised in status to a {{nihongo|National Shrine,3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}  in 1923.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

The shrine is located a 40 minute walk from [[Fukuno Station (Toyama)|Fukuno Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Jōhana Line]].<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>

[https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA371&dq=  ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 371].</ref><ref name="kotodamaya">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/takase-jinja/ "Takase Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=}}'''|天活玉命|}}
*{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-kami|ja|五十猛神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isotakeru-no-kami|qq=}}'''|五十猛神|}}

==History==
The original construction of Takase Jinja is uncertain. Worship of [[Ōkuninushi]] was widespread in the [[Hokuriku region]] from before the [[Nara period]] and is believed to have been brought to this area by migrants from [[Izumo Province|Izumo]]. The shrine claims to have been founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Keikō]] (reigned 71–130 AD) <ref>[http://www.takase.or.jp/guide.html#3] official home page</ref>

The first mention of the shrine in historical records is in an entry in the 780 AD ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the 795 AD ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' it was promoted to Junior 5th Rank, Upper Grade, and the 840 AD ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' states that it was promoted from Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade to Junior 4th Rank, Upper Grade, and per the 854 AD ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', it had been accorded Junior 3rd Rank. Per the 859 AD ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it had advanced to Senior 3rd Rank. While these are indications of its high status, in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD it is listed as only one of seven minor shrines in Tonami County of Etchū Province. However, late in the [[Heian period]], the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Etchū was relocated to Tonami, and the Takase Jinja was elevated to an ''ichinomiya'' and was greatly enlarged. Under the ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' movement joining Shintoism and Buddhism, it once had 300 Buddhist chapels. This period of prosperity was short-lived. With the growth of the ''[[Ikkō-shū]]'' movement in Etchū and the various battles of the [[Sengoku period]], the area around the Takase Jinja was devastated, and it fell largely into ruin. After the establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the creation of [[Toyama Domain]], the shrine was revived with the patronage of the [[Maeda clan]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designed as a "prefectural shrine" under the a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], but was later raised in status to a {{nihongo|National Shrine,3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}  in 1923.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

The shrine is located a 40 minute walk from [[Fukuno Station (Toyama)|Fukuno Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Jōhana Line]].<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>

The shrine claims to have been founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Keikō]] (reigned 71–130 AD) <ref>[http://www.takase.or.jp/guide.html#3] official home page</ref>

The first mention of the shrine in historical records is in an entry in the 780 AD ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the 795 AD ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' it was promoted to Junior 5th Rank, Upper Grade, and the 840 AD ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' states that it was promoted from Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade to Junior 4th Rank, Upper Grade, and per the 854 AD ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', it had been accorded Junior 3rd Rank. Per the 859 AD ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it had advanced to Senior 3rd Rank. While these are indications of its high status, in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD it is listed as only one of seven minor shrines in Tonami County of Etchū Province. However, late in the [[Heian period]], the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Etchū was relocated to Tonami, and the Takase Jinja was elevated to an ''ichinomiya'' and was greatly enlarged. Under the ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' movement joining Shintoism and Buddhism, it once had 300 Buddhist chapels. This period of prosperity was short-lived. With the growth of the ''[[Ikkō-shū]]'' movement in Etchū and the various battles of the [[Sengoku period]], the area around the Takase Jinja was devastated, and it fell largely into ruin. After the establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the creation of [[Toyama Domain]], the shrine was revived with the patronage of the [[Maeda clan]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designed as a "prefectural shrine" under the a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], but was later raised in status to a {{nihongo|National Shrine,3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}  in 1923.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

The shrine is located a 40 minute walk from [[Fukuno Station (Toyama)|Fukuno Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Jōhana Line]].<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>

Under the ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' movement joining Shintoism and Buddhism, it once had 300 Buddhist chapels. This period of prosperity was short-lived. With the growth of the ''[[Ikkō-shū]]'' movement in Etchū and the various battles of the [[Sengoku period]], the area around the Takase Jinja was devastated, and it fell largely into ruin. After the establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the creation of [[Toyama Domain]], the shrine was revived with the patronage of the [[Maeda clan]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designed as a "prefectural shrine" under the a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], but was later raised in status to a {{nihongo|National Shrine,3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}  in 1923.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

The shrine is located a 40 minute walk from [[Fukuno Station (Toyama)|Fukuno Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Jōhana Line]].<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''Takase Shrine'''|高瀬神社|Takase-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Takase neighborhood of the city of [[Nanto, Toyama|Nanto]], [[Toyama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It one of four shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Etchū Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref> The shrine's main festival is held annually on September 13.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

==Enshrined ''kami''==
The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takase Jinja are:
*{{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''|大己貴命|}}.<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA371&dq=  ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 371].</ref><ref name="kotodamaya">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/takase-jinja/ "Takase Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref>

==History==
The original construction of Takase Jinja is uncertain. Worship of [[Ōkuninushi]] was widespread in the [[Hokuriku region]] from before the [[Nara period]] and is believed to have been brought to this area by migrants from [[Izumo Province|Izumo]]. The shrine claims to have been founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Keikō]] (reigned 71–130 AD) <ref>[http://www.takase.or.jp/guide.html#3] official home page</ref>

The first mention of the shrine in historical records is in an entry in the 780 AD ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the 795 AD ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' it was promoted to Junior 5th Rank, Upper Grade, and the 840 AD ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' states that it was promoted from Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade to Junior 4th Rank, Upper Grade, and per the 854 AD ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', it had been accorded Junior 3rd Rank. Per the 859 AD ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it had advanced to Senior 3rd Rank. While these are indications of its high status, in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD it is listed as only one of seven minor shrines in Tonami County of Etchū Province. However, late in the [[Heian period]], the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Etchū was relocated to Tonami, and the Takase Jinja was elevated to an ''ichinomiya'' and was greatly enlarged. Under the ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' movement joining Shintoism and Buddhism, it once had 300 Buddhist chapels. This period of prosperity was short-lived. With the growth of the ''[[Ikkō-shū]]'' movement in Etchū and the various battles of the [[Sengoku period]], the area around the Takase Jinja was devastated, and it fell largely into ruin. After the establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the creation of [[Toyama Domain]], the shrine was revived with the patronage of the [[Maeda clan]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designed as a "prefectural shrine" under the a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], but was later raised in status to a {{nihongo|National Shrine,3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}  in 1923.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

The shrine is located a 40 minute walk from [[Fukuno Station (Toyama)|Fukuno Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Jōhana Line]].<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>

Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designed as a "prefectural shrine" under the a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], but was later raised in status to a {{nihongo|National Shrine,3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}  in 1923.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

The shrine is located a 40 minute walk from [[Fukuno Station (Toyama)|Fukuno Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Jōhana Line]].<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''Takase Shrine'''|高瀬神社|Takase-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Takase neighborhood of the city of [[Nanto, Toyama|Nanto]], [[Toyama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It one of four shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Etchū Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref> The shrine's main festival is held annually on September 13.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

==Enshrined ''kami''==
The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takase Jinja are:
*{{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''|大己貴命|}}.<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA371&dq=  ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 371].</ref><ref name="kotodamaya">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/takase-jinja/ "Takase Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=}}'''|天活玉命|}}
*{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-kami|ja|五十猛神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isotakeru-no-kami|qq=}}'''|五十猛神|}}

==History==
The original construction of Takase Jinja is uncertain. Worship of [[Ōkuninushi]] was widespread in the [[Hokuriku region]] from before the [[Nara period]] and is believed to have been brought to this area by migrants from [[Izumo Province|Izumo]]. The shrine claims to have been founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Keikō]] (reigned 71–130 AD) <ref>[http://www.takase.or.jp/guide.html#3] official home page</ref>

The first mention of the shrine in historical records is in an entry in the 780 AD ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the 795 AD ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' it was promoted to Junior 5th Rank, Upper Grade, and the 840 AD ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' states that it was promoted from Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade to Junior 4th Rank, Upper Grade, and per the 854 AD ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', it had been accorded Junior 3rd Rank. Per the 859 AD ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it had advanced to Senior 3rd Rank. While these are indications of its high status, in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD it is listed as only one of seven minor shrines in Tonami County of Etchū Province. However, late in the [[Heian period]], the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Etchū was relocated to Tonami, and the Takase Jinja was elevated to an ''ichinomiya'' and was greatly enlarged. Under the ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' movement joining Shintoism and Buddhism, it once had 300 Buddhist chapels. This period of prosperity was short-lived. With the growth of the ''[[Ikkō-shū]]'' movement in Etchū and the various battles of the [[Sengoku period]], the area around the Takase Jinja was devastated, and it fell largely into ruin. After the establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the creation of [[Toyama Domain]], the shrine was revived with the patronage of the [[Maeda clan]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designed as a "prefectural shrine" under the a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], but was later raised in status to a {{nihongo|National Shrine,3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}  in 1923.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

The shrine is located a 40 minute walk from [[Fukuno Station (Toyama)|Fukuno Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Jōhana Line]].<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>

The shrine is located a 40 minute walk from [[Fukuno Station (Toyama)|Fukuno Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Jōhana Line]].<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''Takase Shrine'''|高瀬神社|Takase-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Takase neighborhood of the city of [[Nanto, Toyama|Nanto]], [[Toyama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It one of four shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Etchū Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref> The shrine's main festival is held annually on September 13.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

==Enshrined ''kami''==
The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takase Jinja are:
*{{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''|大己貴命|}}.<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA371&dq=  ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 371].</ref><ref name="kotodamaya">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/takase-jinja/ "Takase Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=}}'''|天活玉命|}}
*{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-kami|ja|五十猛神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isotakeru-no-kami|qq=}}'''|五十猛神|}}

==History==
The original construction of Takase Jinja is uncertain. Worship of [[Ōkuninushi]] was widespread in the [[Hokuriku region]] from before the [[Nara period]] and is believed to have been brought to this area by migrants from [[Izumo Province|Izumo]]. The shrine claims to have been founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Keikō]] (reigned 71–130 AD) <ref>[http://www.takase.or.jp/guide.html#3] official home page</ref>

The first mention of the shrine in historical records is in an entry in the 780 AD ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the 795 AD ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' it was promoted to Junior 5th Rank, Upper Grade, and the 840 AD ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' states that it was promoted from Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade to Junior 4th Rank, Upper Grade, and per the 854 AD ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', it had been accorded Junior 3rd Rank. Per the 859 AD ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it had advanced to Senior 3rd Rank. While these are indications of its high status, in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD it is listed as only one of seven minor shrines in Tonami County of Etchū Province. However, late in the [[Heian period]], the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Etchū was relocated to Tonami, and the Takase Jinja was elevated to an ''ichinomiya'' and was greatly enlarged. Under the ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' movement joining Shintoism and Buddhism, it once had 300 Buddhist chapels. This period of prosperity was short-lived. With the growth of the ''[[Ikkō-shū]]'' movement in Etchū and the various battles of the [[Sengoku period]], the area around the Takase Jinja was devastated, and it fell largely into ruin. After the establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the creation of [[Toyama Domain]], the shrine was revived with the patronage of the [[Maeda clan]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designed as a "prefectural shrine" under the a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], but was later raised in status to a {{nihongo|National Shrine,3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}  in 1923.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

The shrine is located a 40 minute walk from [[Fukuno Station (Toyama)|Fukuno Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Jōhana Line]].<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''Takase Shrine'''|高瀬神社|Takase-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Takase neighborhood of the city of [[Nanto, Toyama|Nanto]], [[Toyama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It one of four shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Etchū Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref> The shrine's main festival is held annually on September 13.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

==Enshrined ''kami''==
The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takase Jinja are:
*{{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''|大己貴命|}}.<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA371&dq=  ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 371].</ref><ref name="kotodamaya">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/takase-jinja/ "Takase Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=}}'''|天活玉命|}}
*{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-kami|ja|五十猛神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isotakeru-no-kami|qq=}}'''|五十猛神|}}

==History==
The original construction of Takase Jinja is uncertain. Worship of [[Ōkuninushi]] was widespread in the [[Hokuriku region]] from before the [[Nara period]] and is believed to have been brought to this area by migrants from [[Izumo Province|Izumo]]. The shrine claims to have been founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Keikō]] (reigned 71–130 AD) <ref>[http://www.takase.or.jp/guide.html#3] official home page</ref>

The first mention of the shrine in historical records is in an entry in the 780 AD ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the 795 AD ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' it was promoted to Junior 5th Rank, Upper Grade, and the 840 AD ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' states that it was promoted from Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade to Junior 4th Rank, Upper Grade, and per the 854 AD ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', it had been accorded Junior 3rd Rank. Per the 859 AD ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it had advanced to Senior 3rd Rank. While these are indications of its high status, in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD it is listed as only one of seven minor shrines in Tonami County of Etchū Province. However, late in the [[Heian period]], the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Etchū was relocated to Tonami, and the Takase Jinja was elevated to an ''ichinomiya'' and was greatly enlarged. Under the ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' movement joining Shintoism and Buddhism, it once had 300 Buddhist chapels. This period of prosperity was short-lived. With the growth of the ''[[Ikkō-shū]]'' movement in Etchū and the various battles of the [[Sengoku period]], the area around the Takase Jinja was devastated, and it fell largely into ruin. After the establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the creation of [[Toyama Domain]], the shrine was revived with the patronage of the [[Maeda clan]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designed as a "prefectural shrine" under the a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], but was later raised in status to a {{nihongo|National Shrine,3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}  in 1923.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

The shrine is located a 40 minute walk from [[Fukuno Station (Toyama)|Fukuno Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Jōhana Line]].<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''Takase Shrine'''|高瀬神社|Takase-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Takase neighborhood of the city of [[Nanto, Toyama|Nanto]], [[Toyama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It one of four shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Etchū Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref> The shrine's main festival is held annually on September 13.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

==Enshrined ''kami''==
The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takase Jinja are:
*{{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''|大己貴命|}}.<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA371&dq=  ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 371].</ref><ref name="kotodamaya">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/takase-jinja/ "Takase Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=}}'''|天活玉命|}}
*{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-kami|ja|五十猛神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isotakeru-no-kami|qq=}}'''|五十猛神|}}

==History==
The original construction of Takase Jinja is uncertain. Worship of [[Ōkuninushi]] was widespread in the [[Hokuriku region]] from before the [[Nara period]] and is believed to have been brought to this area by migrants from [[Izumo Province|Izumo]]. The shrine claims to have been founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Keikō]] (reigned 71–130 AD) <ref>[http://www.takase.or.jp/guide.html#3] official home page</ref>

The first mention of the shrine in historical records is in an entry in the 780 AD ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the 795 AD ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' it was promoted to Junior 5th Rank, Upper Grade, and the 840 AD ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' states that it was promoted from Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade to Junior 4th Rank, Upper Grade, and per the 854 AD ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', it had been accorded Junior 3rd Rank. Per the 859 AD ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it had advanced to Senior 3rd Rank. While these are indications of its high status, in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD it is listed as only one of seven minor shrines in Tonami County of Etchū Province. However, late in the [[Heian period]], the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Etchū was relocated to Tonami, and the Takase Jinja was elevated to an ''ichinomiya'' and was greatly enlarged. Under the ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' movement joining Shintoism and Buddhism, it once had 300 Buddhist chapels. This period of prosperity was short-lived. With the growth of the ''[[Ikkō-shū]]'' movement in Etchū and the various battles of the [[Sengoku period]], the area around the Takase Jinja was devastated, and it fell largely into ruin. After the establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the creation of [[Toyama Domain]], the shrine was revived with the patronage of the [[Maeda clan]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designed as a "prefectural shrine" under the a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], but was later raised in status to a {{nihongo|National Shrine,3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}  in 1923.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

The shrine is located a 40 minute walk from [[Fukuno Station (Toyama)|Fukuno Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Jōhana Line]].<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>

| location = 291 Takase, Nanto-shi, Toyama-ken 932-0252
| website = {{Official website|https://www.takase.or.jp}}
| architecture_style = 
| festival = September 13
}}
{{nihongo|'''Takase Shrine'''|高瀬神社|Takase-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Takase neighborhood of the city of [[Nanto, Toyama|Nanto]], [[Toyama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It one of four shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Etchū Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref> The shrine's main festival is held annually on September 13.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

==Enshrined ''kami''==
The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takase Jinja are:
*{{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''|大己貴命|}}.<ref>Pickens, Stuart D. B. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA371&dq=  ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 371].</ref><ref name="kotodamaya">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/takase-jinja/ "Takase Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-25.</ref>
*{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-ikutama-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=}}'''|天活玉命|}}
*{{nihongo|'''{{ill|Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|lt=Isotakeru-no-kami|ja|五十猛神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isotakeru-no-kami|qq=}}'''|五十猛神|}}

==History==
The original construction of Takase Jinja is uncertain. Worship of [[Ōkuninushi]] was widespread in the [[Hokuriku region]] from before the [[Nara period]] and is believed to have been brought to this area by migrants from [[Izumo Province|Izumo]]. The shrine claims to have been founded during the reign of the semi-legendary [[Kofun period]] [[Emperor Keikō]] (reigned 71–130 AD) <ref>[http://www.takase.or.jp/guide.html#3] official home page</ref>

The first mention of the shrine in historical records is in an entry in the 780 AD ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the Rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the 795 AD ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' it was promoted to Junior 5th Rank, Upper Grade, and the 840 AD ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' states that it was promoted from Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade to Junior 4th Rank, Upper Grade, and per the 854 AD ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', it had been accorded Junior 3rd Rank. Per the 859 AD ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it had advanced to Senior 3rd Rank. While these are indications of its high status, in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD it is listed as only one of seven minor shrines in Tonami County of Etchū Province. However, late in the [[Heian period]], the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Etchū was relocated to Tonami, and the Takase Jinja was elevated to an ''ichinomiya'' and was greatly enlarged. Under the ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]'' movement joining Shintoism and Buddhism, it once had 300 Buddhist chapels. This period of prosperity was short-lived. With the growth of the ''[[Ikkō-shū]]'' movement in Etchū and the various battles of the [[Sengoku period]], the area around the Takase Jinja was devastated, and it fell largely into ruin. After the establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and the creation of [[Toyama Domain]], the shrine was revived with the patronage of the [[Maeda clan]]. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designed as a "prefectural shrine" under the a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], but was later raised in status to a {{nihongo|National Shrine,3rd Rank|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}}  in 1923.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

The shrine is located a 40 minute walk from [[Fukuno Station (Toyama)|Fukuno Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Jōhana Line]].<ref name="Shibuya">{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=諸国神社 一宮・二宮・三宮 |date=2015 |publisher=Yamakawa Publishing Company |isbn=978-4634150867}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref><ref name="Irie">{{cite book |last1=Irie |first1=Koichiro |title=諸国一の宮 |date=2018 |publisher=Idō kyōshitsu Publishing |isbn=978-4901398060}}</ref>
